concepts.title: Definition of Intellectual Property Rights (IPR)
concepts.description: Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) refers to the exclusive rights granted to creators and owners of original works, inventions, designs, and other creative expressions to control their use and distribution.

concepts.title: Types of IPR
concepts.description: There are several types of IPR, including patents, trademarks, copyrights, geographical indications, and industrial designs.

concepts.title: Objectives of IPR
concepts.description: The main objectives of IPR are to promote creativity, innovation, and economic growth by providing a framework for the protection of intellectual property and encouraging its development and use.

concepts.title: Mnemonic for remembering the steps in the patent application process
concepts.description: P - Prepare, A - Apply, T - Test, E - Evaluate, N - Notify, T - Transfer, S - Secure: This mnemonic helps to remember the steps involved in the patent application process.

concepts.title: Not registering a trademark
concepts.description: Not registering a trademark can lead to the loss of exclusive rights to use the mark, which can result in financial losses and damage to reputation. It is essential to register a trademark as soon as possible to protect it.

concepts.title: Duration of copyright protection
concepts.description: The duration of copyright protection varies depending on the type of work and the jurisdiction, but generally, it lasts for the life of the author plus a certain number of years (e.g., 50 or 70 years after the author's death).

concepts.title: Importance of IPR in the digital age
concepts.description: In the digital age, IPR plays a crucial role in protecting creative works and inventions from unauthorized use and distribution, which can lead to financial losses and damage to reputation.

concepts.title: International agreements related to IPR
concepts.description: There are several international agreements related to IPR, including the Berne Convention, the Paris Convention, and the TRIPS Agreement, which provide a framework for the protection of intellectual property across borders.
